## [03:00 - 06:03]

The first one is your offer, okay? So how strong is the offer, how unique is it? Are clients actually getting results, right? Closers don't wanna sell people, and then they find out two months later that it's a scam, or they're not getting results, or there's a huge lot of over-promising and under-delivering, or any of that stuff. Also, how many testimonials do you have, okay? How much sales, enablement assets, and content do you have that allows the closers to do a better job in selling, okay? So the first one is a really, really strong offer. The second one is lead flow, okay? Do you have high-quality inbound lead flow? You don't need to be completely inbound reliant, but at the same time, having the opportunity for a closer if they do well, to get at least two, three, four appointments a day coming from inbound, is really, really strong, because a lot of these closers, they can go places where they're gonna get five inbound appointments a day every single day, no prospecting, okay? So I'm totally not, like, I'm all for prospecting. The best closers out there should do both, but you wanna structure your opportunity so it has a little bit of both. The other thing is, is a lead flow quality, okay? Are they quality prospects? Have you demonstrated that you can close these prospects at 25, 30, 40% or more, okay? Because that's gonna show the closer what's actually possible in terms of on-track earnings. And if you're hiring outbound reps, do you have consistent quality streams of opportunity coming in for them to outbound dial or Facebook message or whatever, okay? So a big problem is a lot of people hire on outbound reps and they're just like, hey, just go get me business, okay? That doesn't work, especially because an outbound rep is gonna be typically less skilled than a closer. So you need to have stuff like low ticket products coming in, a Facebook group that's building, a bigger email list, and so you can feed that person quality opportunity that they can go after. So the third thing is comp structure. And more importantly, what are really the on-track earnings? Because comp structure is actually secondary to on-track earnings. On-track earnings is if they hit KPI, what should they be on-track to earn for the year, okay? Now, the easiest way to think about on-track earnings is if you were closing for your own business full-time based on your current comp structure and opportunity volume, what would you be making if your close rates stayed the same? That's the easiest way to find out the contract earnings for your closer. But really the formula for on-track earnings is basically looking at the comp structure, the opportunity volume, and what you've demonstrated being the possible close rate based on the lead quality and figuring out what could a full-time person make for you if they hit all their KPIs. For closers, you really wanna look at $15 to $20,000 a month or more if you're really gonna find the top tier type of people. And last and probably the most important thing is culture. Okay, so are you a rocket-chimp company? How good are you as a leader? Do you communicate the mission vision and values very strongly? Are people bought into the bigger picture? Are you really good at inspiring people? Just remember, the salespeople are the only position in any business that can't sell if they're uninspired. A salesperson will never be able to influence somebody to take action if they're not inspired by the company themselves, okay? So what's the culture like? What's the vibe like? Is it a high-energy type of culture? Do you communicate the vision, mission and values really well? Do they feel like they're bought into something bigger than themselves?
